title: STAT243: Data Analysis and Visualization in R
text:
Preface
This is an interactive trainer for the STAT243 course, “Data Analysis and Visualization in R” at KSE.
For interactivity, the quarto-webr extension for Quarto is used.

title: 01 Basics of R
text:
Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.


Exercise 1.1 Compute the result of 45+35-20 and save it into variable res1. Then multiply it by 2 and save into res2.
Check your answer using check("ex1", res2).

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.



Exercise 1.2 Create a variable weight with your weight (in kg) and height with your height (in meters).
Calculate BMI (weight / height^2) and save it to bmi.
Pass all three variables to the check function.

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.



Exercise 1.3 Check the definition of function round().
What will be the result for 6.8? What if it’s 6.25? What if you specify digits = 1?

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.







TipDiscussion





round() rounds to the nearest even number when the digit is exactly 5 (IEC 60559 standard). That is why round(6.5, 0) might behave unexpectedly compared to school math.





Exercise 1.4 Try to use operator %%. For example 52 %% 5. Guess what this operator does.

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.



Exercise 1.5 What is the class of "5", "TRUE", F, and six (assuming six is a variable)?

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.



Exercise 1.6 What function should be used instead of ____ so the result is TRUE? is.character(____(round(6.2)))

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.



Exercise 1.7 Show the result of c(1,2,3) and 1:3. Check the class of both. Explain the difference.

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.



Exercise 1.8 Create a vector named temp with maximum temperatures: -7, 1, 3, 1, 3, 3, -2.

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.



Exercise 1.9 Calculate the square root of the sum of all elements in the vector temp.

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.



Exercise 1.10 Take the 2nd element of temp, multiply it by the 5th element, and add the 1st element.

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.



Exercise 1.11 Create a vector called weekdays with the names of the days (Monday to Sunday).

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.



Exercise 1.12 Assign names to the temp vector using the weekdays vector. Note: The days in temp correspond to: Tue, Wed, Thu, Fri, Sat, Sun, Mon. You need to reorder weekdays correctly before assigning.

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.



Exercise 1.13 Try functions sort() and order() on temp. What is the difference?

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.



Exercise 1.14 Sort the workers by rank. vec1 are names, vec2 are ranks (1 is best). Create a sorted vector of names called sorted_workers.

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.



Exercise 1.15 Find the maximum, minimum, and average values for vector temp.

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.



Exercise 1.16 Using seq(), generate the sequence 4, 8, 12, 16, 20 and save it into seq1.

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.



Exercise 1.17 Use rev() on seq1 and save the result into seq2.

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.



Exercise 1.18 Combine vectors seq1 and seq2 using c() into a variable seq. Check the result.

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.

title: 02 Key R programming concepts in data analysis | Part 1
text:
Exercise 2.1 Logical Expressions
You’ve got four expressions and two lines of code. Make both of them to be TRUE inputting instead _____ one of the expressions:

23-39
sqrt(16)-16
20-abs(-32)
-(4^2)


_____ == ______
_____ == ______




Exercise 2.2 Logical Vectors
Create a vector logic_vec containing logical values for whether each number in 1:10 is even.



Exercise 2.3 Random Numbers & Names

Create a vector scores of 7 random numbers between -100 and 100. Use function runif().
Assign names to the vector using the days of the week.
Find and display all days where the score is more than -50.


weekdays <- c("Monday","Tuesday","Wednesday","Thursday","Friday","Saturday","Sunday")




Exercise 2.4 Vector Sums
Combine two randomly created vectors vec1 and vec2 of equal length into a new vector vec_sum. Check whether the sum of vec_sum matches the sum of the individual vectors.



Exercise 2.5 Logic & NA (Part 1)
You’ve got a vector with ages: ages <- c(12, 25, 8, 34, 16, 40, 10, 21, 18, 30)

Identify people who are adults (age 18 and above).
Identify teenagers (age between 13 and 17, inclusive).
Replace ages of non-adults with NA.


ages <- c(12, 25, 8, 34, 16, 40, 10, 21, 18, 30)

is_adult <- ages ______ 18
is_teenager <- (ages ______ 13) & (ages ______ 17) 
ages[______] <- NA 




Exercise 2.6 Logic & NA (Part 2)
Continue with the ages vector:

Identify ages that are NA (you can do it with the function is.na()).
Replace missing values (NA) with the mean of the remaining ages.
Create a logical vector to identify people aged 30 and above (is_senior).
Count the number of seniors.


is_missing <- ______(ages)
ages[is_missing] <- ______(ages[!is_missing])  
is_senior <- ages ______ 30  
num_seniors <- ______(is_senior) 




Exercise 2.7 Logic Range
Check if variable x is NOT between 10 (excluding) and 20 (including). Make x to be equal to 20, then to 15, then to 25 to test your logic.



Exercise 2.8 Comparing Vectors
Use a logical vector to identify elements in vec1 that are either equal or greater than their corresponding values in vec2. Display these elements for vec1. Do it with a single line of code.

vec1 <- c(10, 20, 30, 40, 50)
vec2 <- c(50, 40, 30, 20, 10)




Exercise 2.9 If Condition
Write an IF condition to check if the sum of elements in vec1 is positive. - If so, print “Positive balance”. - Otherwise, print “Negative balance”.

vec1 <- c(100, -50, -20)




Exercise 2.10 Condition Chains
Write an IF condition for a variable temperature. Let temperature <- 30.

If it’s greater than 25, print “Hot”.
If it’s between 10 and 25 (inclusive), print “Moderate”.
Otherwise, print “Cold”.


temperature <- 30




Exercise 2.11 Ifelse & Coin Toss
Simulate a coin toss (1 for heads, 0 for tails). Use ifelse() to assign “Win” for heads and “Lose” for tails in a vector of 1000 random coin tosses.



Exercise 2.12 Nested Ifelse (BMI)
Given a vector with BMI, create a new vector using ifelse() to classify BMI as:

“Good” (less than 25)
“Moderate obesity” (25 to 35 including)
“Obesity” (more than 35)


bmi <- c(24.4,36.7,38.0,36.9,30.8,34.8,31.8,26.7,36.7,34.9,36.2,24.9,26.6,32.7,24.6,30.8,30.6,38.6,26.2,26.6)




Exercise 2.13 Matrices
Create a 3x3 matrix with random numbers between 1 and 100 and save to mat.



Exercise 2.14 Matrix Indexing
Replace all numbers greater than 50 with their half using matrix indexing on mat.



Exercise 2.15 Named Matrices
Create a named 2x3 matrix representing sales data for two products across three months. Calculate the total sales for each month and each product.



Exercise 2.16 Identity Matrix

Create a 4x4 identity matrix. Use function diag() for this.
Replace its diagonal with the numbers 1, 4, 9, 16.




Exercise 2.17 Matrix Analysis (TikTok)
Create a dataset with 5 tiktok users (rows) and 3 weeks (columns) with amount of tiktoks watched. You may use runif again, if you’d like to.
Make a single line of code to print in console the answer to: 1. The week with the highest total tiktoks watched. 2. The user with the highest average tiktoks watched per week.
crumbs: 2  02 Key R programming concepts in data analysis | Part 1

title: 03 Key R programming concepts in data analysis | Part 2
text:
Exercise 3.1 Factors: Creation
Create a vector vec for outfit ratings: “Casual”, “Drip”, “Drip”, “Casual”, “Drip”, “Slay”, “Drip”, “Slay”, “Casual”, “Drip”, “Casual”, “Slay”.
Convert it into a factor fact with levels “Casual”, “Drip”, “Slay”.

vec <- c("Casual", "Drip", "Drip", "Casual", "Drip", "Slay", 
         "Drip", "Slay", "Casual", "Drip", "Casual", "Slay")
# Your code here




Exercise 3.2 Factors: Renaming Levels
Replace the levels “Casual”, “Drip”, “Slay” with “C”, “D”, “S” in the factor you created previously.

# Your code here




Exercise 3.3 Factors: Adding Levels (*)
Add a new factor level “Cringe” to the factor you created previously and add two elements “Cringe” in the end.
Hint: For this, you need to recreate fact as character, combine with 2 “Cringe”s and put in levels the levels of previously created fact. Do the addition of elements in a single line of code.

# Your code here




Exercise 3.4 Factors: Specific Rename
Rename only “Cringe” level of the factor to “Cr”.

# Your code here




Exercise 3.5 Factors: Summary
Generate a summary of the factor you created previously.

# Your code here




Exercise 3.6 Simulation: Coin Toss

Simulate a coin toss (1 for heads, 0 for tails).
Use ifelse() to assign “Win” for heads and “Lose” for tails in a vector of 1000 random coin tosses.
Make it a factor and then show the summary.


# Your code here




Exercise 3.7 Data Frames: Creation
Create a data frame city_ua with the following vectors:

City: “Kyiv”, “Lviv”, “Odesa”, “Kharkiv”, “Dnipro”
Population (millions): 2.9, 0.7, 1.0, 1.4, 1.0
Subway: TRUE, FALSE, FALSE, TRUE, TRUE

Extract the Population of “Odesa” in a matrix style.

# Your code here




Exercise 3.8 Data Frames: Filtering
Extract all the rows where the population is greater than 1 million. Do it both in data.frame style and in matrix style.

# Your code here




Exercise 3.9 Data Frames: Logical Subset
Create a subset of cities that do not have subway systems. Do it in both data frame style and matrix style.
Challenge: To make things funnier, in your line of code don’t use TRUE, T, FALSE, F :)

# Your code here




Exercise 3.10 **Data Frames: New Column (*)**
Add a new column to the data frame indicating whether the city is located in Eastern Ukraine (TRUE for “Kharkiv” and “Dnipro”, FALSE otherwise).
Try the solution without and with the operator %in% to check how it works.

Example of how %in% works: vec %in% c("Cringe","Slay") will give a logical vector indicating elements that are either “Cringe” or “Slay”.


# Your code here




Exercise 3.11 Data Frames: Calculation
Calculate the mean population of cities that have a subway.

# Your code here




Exercise 3.12 Data Analysis: Loading Data
Load the diabetes.csv into diab and print the summary of the data. Discuss.

# Your code here




Exercise 3.13 Data Analysis: Basic Operations
Do the following actions with a line of code for each:

Find the mean of the BMI column in the dataset.
Select all rows where the Age is greater than 50.
Select all patients with Age more than 50 and diabetes.


# Your code here




Exercise 3.14 Data Analysis: Conditional Factor
Create a new factor column bmi_level in the dataset that indicates whether the BMI is greater than 25 (label as “High BMI” or “Normal BMI”).

# Your code here




Exercise 3.15 Data Analysis: Group Comparison
In the diabetes dataset, compare the average BMI of those who have diabetes and those who don’t.

# Your code here




Exercise 3.16 Data Analysis: Sorting
Create sorted diabetes by increasing BMI. Check average Outcome in the first half and in the second half of the dataset. Explain the result.

# Your code here




Exercise 3.17 Control Flow: While Loop
Create a while loop where i starts at 26. Print “i is not divisible by 11” (where i is a current number) in each iteration and stop if the square is divisible by 11. After the loop print “i is divisible by 11” using final “i”.






Tip





You may want to use paste0() function in this task.




# Your code here




Exercise 3.18 Data Frames: Student Grades
Create your own dataframe with students that attend these seminars. You need at least 7 students.
Then you need columns:

Name (up to you)
Exam I score, Exam II score (put some grades from 0 to 20)
Column “passed”, factor with values “y” or “n”. Fill it with “y” if total grade is >=30, “n” otherwise.

Tasks:

Reorder dataset from top student to “not that top” by Exam II.
Calculate mean and max score for Exam I.
Show student names who has top grade in each exam and overall.
Adjust dataset values in a way to have three different students in output 🙂


# Your code here
crumbs: 3  03 Key R programming concepts in data analysis | Part 2

title: 04 Advanced R programming concepts
text:
Exercise 4.1 Loops: Cumulative Sum

Create a vector vec with 100 elements from 0 to 10 using runif().
Write a for loop to find the cumulative sum of vec and store it in a vector called cumul.


# Your code here




Exercise 4.2 Functions: Basic Creation
Write a function cube() that takes a number and returns its cube (). Test it on numbers: 4, -2.

# Your code here




Exercise 4.3 Functions: Arguments & Logic
Modify the cube() function to allow for a logical argument show_info (default FALSE).

If show_info is TRUE, the function should print: “x to the power of 3 equal to y” (where x and y are actual numbers).
Test on 4 with T and -2 with F.


# Your code here




Exercise 4.4 Lists: Creation
Create a list called student containing three elements:

A name (character).
Scores (numeric vector).
A boolean passed indicating whether the average score is .


# Your code here




Exercise 4.5 Lists: Summary
Write a function list_summary() that takes a list and returns its element names and types (classes). Test it on the list student made previously.

# Your code here




Exercise 4.6 Apply Family: lapply

Create a dataset temp_cities of city temperatures for five days in five cities.
Use lapply() to calculate the mean temperature for each city.


# Your code here




Exercise 4.7 Apply Family: sapply

Write a function day_range() that calculates the temperature range (max - min) for a vector.
Use sapply() to apply it to temp_cities.


# Your code here




Exercise 4.8 Functions: Custom Statistics
Write a function custom_summary() that calculates the mean, median, and standard deviation of a numeric vector. Test it on vec (from Exercise 1 or a new one).

# Your code here




Exercise 4.9 Functions: Normalization

Create a function normalize() that takes a numeric vector and rescales its values between 0 and 1. Formula: .
Use sapply() to normalize each city in temp_cities.


# Your code here




Exercise 4.10 Data Import: Read Excel
Use function read_excel() from the library readxl to load the urbanpop.xlsx file and save it into pop_1.

Note: Don’t forget about setwd() routine or use the full path to the file.


# Your code here




Exercise 4.11 Data Import: Sheets

Use excel_sheets() on urbanpop.xlsx. Discuss your findings.
Try to load the second sheet into pop_2 using both its number (index) and its name.


# Your code here




Exercise 4.12 Data Import: Bulk Loading (*)
Use lapply function to load data from urbanpop.xlsx file, from multiple sheets, into a list called pop_list.

# Your code here




Exercise 4.13 Data Manipulation: Merging
Merge the 3 dataframes from pop_list using the cbind() function into a single dataframe df.
Constraint: Get rid of unnecessary columns “country” from the 2nd and 3rd datasets inside the cbind function when you’re doing the merge.

# Your code here




Exercise 4.14 Data Cleaning: Regex & Formatting
We’ve got data in a creepy format with “k” (thousands) and “M” (millions) instead of numbers.

Save the second column from df into vec.
Use gsub() to change “k” to “e3” and “M” to “e6” (scientific format).
Convert them to numeric and save into vec_num.


# Your code here




Exercise 4.15 Data Cleaning: Automation
Use the logic from the previous solution to apply this cleaning to the df dataframe and make the columns numeric.

# Your code here
crumbs: 4  04 Advanced R programming concepts

title: 05 Descriptive statistics | Part 1 (EDA)
text:
Exercise 5.1 Data Import & Overview
Load responses.csv to df and show the summary. Then show all the column names and the first couple of rows, and discuss about the dataset.

# Your code here




Exercise 5.2 Categorical Data: Summary
Give us a summary about Gender. How to make it useful? What function should be used on df$Gender? Discuss why this variable may be useful in the research.

# Your code here




Exercise 5.3 Data Interpretation & Plotting
Use summary() for spiders column. Try to interpret each result. Then make plots with plot() for both.

# Your code here




Exercise 5.4 Subsetting by Groups
Do the summary for spiders, but for different genders. Make plots as well. For this you may’d like to create a subset. Discuss the result.

# Your code here




Exercise 5.5 Repeating Analysis
Repeat the previous steps (summary, subsetting, and plotting) for Height and then for Weight. Discuss results.

# Your code here




Exercise 5.6 Calculating Proportions
Calculate the proportion of respondents who love music (values 4-5).

# Your code here




Exercise 5.7 Feature Engineering: Faith
Find how religious the youth is. Check variables God and Religion, create a new variable Faith which is equal to the sum of God and Religion divided by 2, rounded to the bottom with floor(). Check it with summary. Check the percentage of unreligious (1-2) people who believe in God (4-5).

# Your code here




Exercise 5.8 Advanced Summaries & sapply
You’d like to show all mins, maxs, means and medians. For this you have to work with numeric columns only to avoid errors. Using sapply and function is.numeric() find all numeric columns. Then subset df with only those columns into df_num.

# Your code here




Exercise 5.9 Applying Functions to Dataframes (*)
Continue with df_num. Use sapply with functions min/max/mean/median and na.rm=T and then explain the result.

# Your code here

title: 06 Data Cleaning and Correlation Analysis
text:
Exercise 6.1 Outlier Detection: IQR Rule
Load the responses dataset into df. Use the IQR Rule to identify potential outliers for Weight and Height.






Note



Do not rewrite or modify the original data yet. You may save df$Weight into a variable wght and df$Height into hght for convenience. Discuss the results of the detection.



# Your code here




Exercise 6.2 Missing Values: Mean Imputation
For the wght variable (Weight), fill all NA values with the mean value.






Tip



You might want to use the round() function so the mean becomes an integer, matching the style of other observations.



# Your code here




Exercise 6.3 Missing Values: Group-based Imputation

Convert the Gender column into a factor.
Repeat the NA filling process for hght (Height), but this time do it according to the gender (use the mean height of males for missing male values, and vice versa).
Check if there is anything wrong or unusual in the process.


# Your code here




Exercise 6.4 Handling Anomalies: Weight
Find anomalies in wght using the IQR Rule. This time, rewrite (replace) the identified weight anomalies with the overall mean.

# Your code here




Exercise 6.5 Handling Anomalies: Height
Find anomalies in hght using the IQR Rule. Rewrite these anomalies with the mean of the corresponding gender.

# Your code here




Exercise 6.6 Data Cleaning: Listwise Deletion
Use the na.omit() function on the entire dataframe.

How many observations are left?
How should we interpret this result in the context of data loss?


# Your code here




Exercise 6.7 Correlation: Physical Attributes
Check the correlation between df$Height and df$Weight.

Does the result look correct?
If not, check the correlation between the cleaned variables hght and wght and discuss the difference.


# Your code here




Exercise 6.8 Correlation: Beliefs
Check the correlation between the variables God and Religion.

To do this, you first need to handle NA values (either fill them or remove them).
For this task, let’s try removing them.


# Your code here




Exercise 6.9 Correlation Matrix
Create a correlation matrix for all variables in the dataset.

Step 1: Filter the dataframe to include only numeric variables.
Step 2: Use na.omit() to handle missing values.
Step 3: Save the result into a matrix called cormat.


# Your code here




Exercise 6.10 Visualization: Corrplot
Install and load the corrplot library. Use the corrplot() function to visualize your cormat. Discuss the patterns you observe.

# Your code here




Exercise 6.11 Matrix Operations: Finding Extremes
Find the minimum value in the cormat (the strongest negative correlation).

Use the which() function with the parameter arr.ind = TRUE to find the exact row and column indices where the correlation equals the minimum value of the matrix.
Discuss what these two variables represent.


# Your code here
crumbs: 6  06 Data Cleaning and Correlation Analysis

title: 07 Data Manipulation with dplyr
text:
Exercise 7.1 Data Import & Overview

Load necessary library library(tidyverse).
Load data from counties.csv using function read_csv() instead of the read.csv().
Show summary, first few rows and column names.
Discuss what is this dataset about.


# Your code here




Exercise 7.2 Pipe Notation
Rewrite in a pipe notation following function:
sort(unique(counties$state),decreasing=T)

# Your code here




Exercise 7.3 Selecting Columns
Select only the columns state, county, and population from counties using select() function. Write into counties_selected.

# Your code here




Exercise 7.4 Excluding Columns
Select all columns except population and region from counties. Use “-”.

# Your code here




Exercise 7.5 Arranging Data
Arrange counties in descending order based on private_work percentage. Using arrange() and desc() functions.

# Your code here




Exercise 7.6 Filtering Data
Filter counties where metro is Metro and population is greater than 500,000 using filter().

# Your code here




Exercise 7.7 Creating Columns (Mutate)
Create a new column total_workforce as the sum of private_work and public_work percentages.

# Your code here




Exercise 7.8 Multiple Filters
Filter counties that belong to the South region and have a population under 50000.

# Your code here




Exercise 7.9 Calculating New Variables
Create a new variable self_employed_pop as the number of self-employed individuals.

# Your code here




Exercise 7.10 Finding Top Observations
Find the county with the highest percentage of public workers. You may use head() to take only top observation.

# Your code here




Exercise 7.11 Filter and Sort
Filter counties where more than 60% of the population is male and sort by population descending.

# Your code here




Exercise 7.12 Complex Pipeline
For counties in a single pipe select variables state, county, region, population, men, women, citizens. Then filter that there are more men than women and number of citizens is more than 10000 and the region is West or NorthEast. Then arrange by citizens in descending order.

# Your code here




Exercise 7.13 Subsetting and Mutating Pipeline
From counties select state, county, population, men, women, self_employed, add the proportion of men called proportion_men, take only counties with Proportion of men bigger than 60%, sort by ProportionMen in descending order and save into counties_tmp.

# Your code here




Exercise 7.14 Modifying Existing Columns
In counties_tmp remake proportion_men not as %, but as decimal with a function mutate().

# Your code here




Exercise 7.15 Debugging Code
Why the following doesn’t work?

counties %>%
  select(state, county, population, men, women, self_employed)  %>%
  transmute(proportion_men=men/population*100) %>%
  filter(proportion_men>60) %>%
  arrange(desc(self_employed))




Exercise 7.16 Using Transmute
Use transmute over counties in a way to leave state, county, population and create proportion_citizens. Save into counties_tmp.

# Your code here




Exercise 7.17 Proportions and Filtering
Compute the proportion of self-employed individuals and filter only counties where it’s above 10%, then sort by proportion descending.

# Your code here




Exercise 7.18 End-to-end Pipeline
Create a pipeline that selects relevant columns, filters counties with at least 50000 population, creates a new variable for the male-to-female ratio, and sorts by this ratio.

# Your code here




Exercise 7.19 Advanced Pipeline
Select state, county, population, private_work, public_work, self_employed, compute into total_employment the total percentage of employment by summing up private, public, and self-employment percentages, then filter only counties where it’s below 98% from Texas and arrange by descending order.

# Your code here
crumbs: 7  07 Data Manipulation with dplyr

title: 08 Data Aggregation and Grouping with dplyr
text:
Important




Load necessary library library(tidyverse).
Load data from counties.csv.




Exercise 8.1 Counting Categories
Count the number of counties in each metro/non-metro category.

# Your code here




Exercise 8.2 Conditional Counting
Count the number of counties in each state where the average household income is above 60,000.

# Your code here




Exercise 8.3 Weighted Counting
Count the number of counties in each state, weighted by the number of employed people, and sort in descending order.

# Your code here




Exercise 8.4 Multi-group Counting
Count the number of metro and non-metro counties per state.

# Your code here




Exercise 8.5 Average Percentage (Two Ways)
Find the average percentage of white people throughout all counties. Propose 2 approaches, one with mean() and another with summarize().

# Your code here




Exercise 8.6 Conditional Average
Find the average percentage of white people throughout all counties, which have income more than 70,000.

# Your code here




Exercise 8.7 Finding the Maximum
Find the state with the maximum average household income.

# Your code here




Exercise 8.8 Finding the Minimum Amount
Find the county with the lowest amount of people working in the public sector.

# Your code here




Exercise 8.9 Summarizing Totals
Compute the total number of unemployed people in each state and sort in descending order.

# Your code here




Exercise 8.10 Mean and Median
Find the average and median number of people per county working in private companies.

# Your code here




Exercise 8.11 Calculating Percentages
Calculate the percentage of the population that works in construction per state.

# Your code here




Exercise 8.12 Grouped Maximum
Find the state with the highest average unemployment rate among metro counties.

# Your code here




Exercise 8.13 Multiple Metrics
Compute the total number of counties per state and the average number of people walking to work per state.

# Your code here




Exercise 8.14 Highest Proportion
Find the state with the highest proportion of people working in self-employment.

# Your code here




Exercise 8.15 Proportion of Citizens
Compute the proportion of people in each state who are U.S. citizens.

# Your code here




Exercise 8.16 Complex Pipeline
For the counties dataset do the following:

Select variables state, population, white, black, hispanic, native, asian.
Filter for counties where there are more than 20% of black persons, more than 10% of hispanic persons, and the population is bigger than 150,000.
Show the total amount (sum) of black, hispanic, native, and asian people by state.


# Your code here
crumbs: 8  08 Data Aggregation and Grouping with dplyr

title: 09 Tidying Data and Joins (tidyr & dplyr)
text:
Exercise 9.1 Pivoting Wider: Basic
Convert the counties dataset into a wide format where the columns are county, state, metro or non-metro (after pivoting) and values are population. Do it with pivot_wider().

# Your code here




Exercise 9.2 Pivoting Wider: Aggregation
Convert the counties dataset into a wide format where for each state the population is calculated by metro and non-metro. So we’ll have columns state, metro, nonmetro and 50 rows for each state.

# Your code here




Exercise 9.3 Pivoting Wider: States as Columns
Convert the counties dataset into a wide format where each state becomes a column and the values are the total population in that state. Save into counties_pop_wide.

# Your code here




Exercise 9.4 Pivoting Longer
Convert the wide-format data back into a long format where the state names become a column again from counties_pop_wide.

# Your code here




Exercise 9.5 Summary and Pivoting
Create a summary table showing the average income in each metro and non-metro area per region, then pivot it wider by metro and save into counties_reg_metro.

# Your code here




Exercise 9.6 Joining Data: left_join
Create another table by region for average income calculated correctly, save it into counties_reg_tot and connect it to counties_reg_metro with the left_join() and resave the result as the counties_reg_tot.

# Your code here




Exercise 9.7 Complex Pipeline: Population & Income (Part 1)
Create a total population by region and metro, make it wider to have metro and nonmetro columns, save into counties_reg_metro_pop. Then do the same but just by region and save into counties_metro_pop.

# Your code here




Exercise 9.8 Complex Pipeline: Population & Income (Part 2)
To counties_reg_metro_pop join the counties_metro_pop and calculate the share of metro population in total population, same for nonmetro. Then join the counties_reg_tot by region and give suffixes _perc and _inc for percentages and income. At the last stage, compare mean_inc with mean_inc_new, which is calculated as mean income for metro and non-metro multiplied by the percentage of population in metro and non-metro area. Are they the same? Why?

# Your code here




Exercise 9.9 Joining External Data
Perform a left join between the counties dataset and a dataset containing state abbreviations (e.g., state.abb and state.name from base R), ensuring that no counties are lost in the process.

# Your code here




Exercise 9.10 Cross Tabulation & Pivoting
Create a table with percentage of white, black, and other people (columns) and metro / non-metro county (rows).

# Your code here




Exercise 9.11 Calculations and Joins
Find the population density for each state and the average income for each state, then join them. Check the correlation between two.

# Your code here
crumbs: 9  09 Tidying Data and Joins (tidyr & dplyr)

title: 10 Data Manipulation with IMDb Dataset
text:
Exercise 10.1 Data Loading and Joining
Load ImdbTitleBasics and ImdbTitleRatings into imdb_1 and imdb_2. Check column names and first few rows. Join imdb_1 and imdb_2 into imdb, and remove imdb_1 and imdb_2.

library(tidyverse)
library(googledrive)

# 1. Specify the full URLs
link_1 <- "https://drive.google.com/file/d/1FzYor9a8oY-6GKQAkbUGF4xO87nAlVmT/view?usp=drive_link"
link_2 <- "https://drive.google.com/file/d/1r4mHgnORXQVPs6ugUDMZoaY9vT4rc2fG/view?usp=drive_link"

# (Optional) If the files are publicly accessible via a link, this command 
# will allow you to download them WITHOUT logging in (R will not open a browser window asking you to log in)
drive_deauth()

# 2. Upload the files. as_id() will automatically retrieve the required ID from the link
drive_download(as_id(link_1), path = "ImdbTitleBasics.csv", overwrite = TRUE)
drive_download(as_id(link_2), path = "ImdbTitleRatings.csv", overwrite = TRUE)

# 3. Reading local files
imdb_1 <- read_csv("ImdbTitleBasics.csv")
imdb_2 <- read_csv("ImdbTitleRatings.csv")








TipOr alternativly






library(tidyverse)
library(googledrive)

drive_deauth()

# Creating vectors with links and filenames
links <- c(
  "https://drive.google.com/file/d/1FzYor9a8oY-6GKQAkbUGF4xO87nAlVmT/view?usp=drive_link",
  "https://drive.google.com/file/d/1r4mHgnORXQVPs6ugUDMZoaY9vT4rc2fG/view?usp=drive_link"
)

filenames <- c("ImdbTitleBasics.csv", "ImdbTitleRatings.csv")

# We automatically iterate through both vectors and download each file
walk2(links, filenames, ~drive_download(as_id(.x), path = .y, overwrite = TRUE))

# Reading Files
imdb_1 <- read_csv(filenames[1])
imdb_2 <- read_csv(filenames[2])






Exercise 10.2 Data Cleaning
Clean the imdb a little bit. First check the unique genres in the dataset, discuss. Then remove all the movies where the “Adult” in genres (use str_detect() for it) and also remake startYear, endYear and runtimeMinutes as numeric.

# Your code here




Exercise 10.3 Top Movies by Runtime
Show top-2 movies by the runtime in each genre, checking only titles with 1 genre. Tip: If there are 2+ genres, what symbol will definitely be in genres? Tip 2: You may need function slice_head() to take 2 titles from each group.

# Your code here




Exercise 10.4 Sorting by Decades
Sort in descending order title average scores for each decade, taking only titles with 1k+ votes.

# Your code here




Exercise 10.5 Counting by Format
For movies and tvseries make a table 1x2 with amount of movies and TV series.

# Your code here




Exercise 10.6 Most Voted per Year
Find the most voted title for each year.

# Your code here




Exercise 10.7 Rating Distributions
Compare the rating distribution of movies and TV series. Calculate Mean, median and standard deviation.

# Your code here




Exercise 10.8 Separating Rows by Genres
Use separate_rows() on genres with sep="," to divide titles with multiple genres into multiple rows where each observation may appear multiple times, but with different genres. Check the amount of observations in this case. Compare with the amount of observations of just imdb and the case where you leave only titles with a single genre. Now check unique genres for imdb with separated rows and compare.

# Your code here

title: 11 Data Visualization: Scatter Plots, Histograms, and Bar Charts
text:
Exercise 11.1 Scatter plot with log GDP and continent colors
Create a scatter plot of logarithmized GDP per capita vs Life Expectancy, colored by continent. Use point size 2.

library(tidyverse)
library(gapminder)

gpmind <- gapminder

# Your code here




Exercise 11.2 Scatter plot with population-based size
Create a scatter plot of log GDP per capita vs Life Expectancy with color by continent, size based on population, and transparency 0.6.

# Your code here




Exercise 11.3 Scatter plot for 2007 with minimal theme
Create a scatter plot with log GDP per capita and Life Expectancy colored by continent for year 2007 only. Use point size 5 and add theme_minimal().

# Your code here




Exercise 11.4 Scatter plot with smooth trend lines
Create a scatter plot with smooth trend lines per continent. Use both geom_point() (alpha=0.6, size=3) and geom_smooth(se=F) with line width 3.

# Your code here




Exercise 11.5 Scatter plot with country labels
Create a scatter plot with country labels instead of dots. Use geom_text() with log GDP per capita on x-axis and Life Expectancy on y-axis, colored by continent.

# Your code here




Exercise 11.6 Annotated scatter plot with year labels
Calculate average life expectancy and average GDP per capita by year and continent. Create a scatter plot with year labels using a combination of geom_point() and geom_text(vjust=-0.5). Add theme_minimal().

# Your code here




Exercise 11.7 Histogram of Life Expectancy in 2007
Create a histogram of Life Expectancy in 2007 with binwidth=3, filled with "lightblue3" and black border.

# Your code here




Exercise 11.8 Histogram filled by continent
Create a histogram of Life Expectancy filled by continent with binwidth=1, black border, and transparency 0.7.

# Your code here




Exercise 11.9 Density plot by continent
Create a density plot of Life Expectancy in 2007 by continent with transparency 0.3.

# Your code here




Exercise 11.10 Bar chart of countries per continent
Create a bar chart of the number of countries per continent in 2007. Try to do it using both geom_col() and geom_bar(). In which case did you succeed?

# Your code here




Exercise 11.11 Bar chart: weighted vs simple mean
Create a bar chart of average life expectancy by continent in 2007, comparing weighted average (sum(lifeExp*pop)/sum(pop)) and simple mean. Use pivot_longer() and position = "dodge".

# Your code here




Exercise 11.12 Median GDP bar chart
Create a bar chart of median GDP per capita by continent in 2007, filled with "lightblue3".

# Your code here




Exercise 11.13 Grouped bar chart by year
Create a bar chart of maximum life expectancy by continent for years 1997, 2002, and 2007. Use position="dodge" and color by year (as factor).

# Your code here

title: 12 Data Visualization: Line Charts, Area Charts, and Facets
text:
Exercise 12.1 Line chart for a single country
Create a line chart of life expectancy over time for Germany.

library(tidyverse)
library(gapminder)

gpmind <- gapminder

# Your code here




Exercise 12.2 Weighted average life expectancy by continent
Create a line chart of weighted average life expectancy over time for all continents. Use line width 2 and add theme_bw().

# Your code here




Exercise 12.3 Line chart with points for Japan
Create a line chart with points showing GDP per capita over time for Japan. Line width 1, point size 3 (use size 5 for visibility), color "darkblue" for both, and theme_bw().

# Your code here




Exercise 12.4 Area chart for Africa
Create an area chart of total population over time for Africa, filled with "lightblue3", transparency 0.5, and theme_bw().

# Your code here




Exercise 12.5 Line chart with smooth trend for Rwanda
Create a line chart of GDP per capita for Rwanda with a smooth trend line. The main line should have width 2 and color "darkblue". The smoothed line should have no standard errors, color "lightblue3", and dashed line type. Add theme_bw().

# Your code here




Exercise 12.6 Stacked area chart with custom colors
Create a stacked area chart of population by continent over time with transparency 0.9 and theme_bw(). Change the color palette using scale_fill_manual() with values: "#003964", "#00bbce", "#A7C539", "#D33E2C", "#00665a".

# Your code here




Exercise 12.7 Multi-country line chart with custom colors
Create a line chart of GDP per capita for USA, China, France, and Germany. Use theme_bw(), line width 2, and palette "#003964", "#00bbce", "#A7C539", "#D33E2C".

# Your code here




Exercise 12.8 Reorder legend with factor levels
Take the previous chart and change the legend order to: Germany, France, China, United States. Use factor() with custom levels.

# Your code here




Exercise 12.9 Faceted line chart by continent
Create a faceted line chart of life expectancy over time, with one panel per continent. Use facet_wrap(~continent), line width 1.5, and color by continent. Don’t forget the group=country aesthetic.

# Your code here




Exercise 12.10 Median vs weighted average GDP
Create a line chart of median and weighted average GDP per capita by continent over time. Differentiate the two measures by line type. Use pivot_longer(), line width 1.5, and theme_bw().

# Your code here




Exercise 12.11 GDP ratio between two countries
Create a line chart of the ratio of GDP per capita between Germany and China over time. Use pivot_wider() to reshape the data, line width 1.5, and theme_bw().

# Your code here




Exercise 12.12 Annual GDP growth rate
Create a line chart of the average annual GDP growth rates (in %) over time for Australia. Use the lag() function to access the previous value. Line width 1.5 and theme_bw().

# Your code here
